content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 11, 2000

Barbara A. Stewart
Post Office Box 825
Dorris, CA 96023

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-262

Dear Ms. Stewart:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit you from applying for and
receiving a Community Development Block Grant?

CONCLUSION

No. You are not prohibited from applying for and receiving a Community
Development Block Grant since you are not making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision.

_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-262
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Clerk for the City of Dorris and have applied for a Community
Development Block Grant ("CDBG") loan. You do not vote on any decisions for the
City, only the city council members vote. You do attest to signatures, such as the
Mayor's signature, on contracts with Great Northern, with whom the CDBG has
contracted to manage their money. You are not on the bank signature card for the
account on which checks are written for the CDBG loans. You have no other
responsibilities related to these loans.

The property for which you are applying for a CDBG loan is rental property with
a 1974 singlewide mobile home. Your son, who is developmentally disabled, now rents
the property from you and your husband.

Mr. Larry Luce, the CDBG program manager, informed you that you and your
husband would be eligible for a loan on rental property since your renter was disabled.
He advised that he inquired and was told by his superiors that you could apply even
though you were City Clerk for the City of Dorris.

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.

Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 of the Act
requires analysis of the following questions²:

Are you a "public official" within the meaning of the rules?

As City Clerk of the City of Dorris, you are a "member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, are subject to the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)

___________________________
² These questions are based on the Act's conflict of interest analysis provided at Regulation
18700(b). The Commission document "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview" explains the steps of
this analysis and is enclosed for your information.

File No. A-00-262
Page No. 3

Will you make, participate in making, or influence a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A
public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision maker
regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision before his
or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section
87100; Regulation 18702.3.)

Making or participating in making a governmental decision does not include
actions of public officials which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical.
(Regulation 18702.4(a)(1).) Additionally, an official is not attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision of an agency if the official appears
in the same manner as any other member of the general public before an agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself or herself on a
matter which is related to his or her personal interests.³ (Regulation 18702.4(b)(1).)

As you have described them, your duties with respect to CDBG loans consist of
attesting to signatures such as the Mayor's, on contracts with Great Northern, the entity
administering the CDBGs loan program. We have previously advised that duties carried
out by the treasurer of the City of Dorris which were related to processing a CDBG loan
are either ministerial or clerical in nature because the duties did not require the exercise
of discretion. (Tripp Advice Letter, No. A-92-056.) Because your responsibility to attest
to the signatures on the contracts entails even less involvement with the CDBG loan
processing, we conclude that your duties are similarly ministerial actions. As such, these
duties do not constitute making or participating in making a governmental decision.
Additionally, based on the facts you provided, you also are not influencing a
governmental decision as long as you apply for a CDBG loan in the same manner as any
other member of the general public.⁴ Since you are not making, participating in making,
or influencing a governmental decision, the conflict of interest rules do not prohibit you
from applying for and receiving a CDBG loan.

___________________________
³ "Personal interests" include an interest in real property which is wholly owned by the official or
members of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18702.4(b)(1)(A).)

⁴ The exception of Regulation 18702.4(b)(1) will not apply if you contact, appear before, or
otherwise attempt to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency, such as Mr.
Luce, in a manner different from one available to any other member of the general public. (Regulations
18702.3(a) and 18702.4(b)(1).)

File No. A-00-262
Page No. 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

NB:jg
Enclosure
